heading: Cantico la sposa purissima è paragonata dallo sposo a una colomba:
card_summary: This section of the Secondo Cantico (Book Two, Tono Terzo) of De Harmonia Mundi develops an extended allegorical reading of Levitical purification rites, centred on the dove as a symbol of the Holy Spirit and of Christ the Purifier. Zorzi interprets the two birds of the leper's cleansing ritual (Leviticus 14) as figures for Christ's spiritual and animal natures, drawing on Origen's commentaries, Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, and Isaiah, with substantial Hebrew philology (tzipporim, tola'at, shani, tzo'n, keves). The goat sacrificed for sin symbolises lascivious concupiscence; the bull symbolises pride, both mapped onto the Platonic/Origenist schema of the irascible and concupiscible faculties. The section closes with a meditation on sheep and lambs as types of Christ the Redeemer, praising the great biblical shepherds from Abel to David, and introducing Hebrew vocabulary for the ovine family (tzo'n, keves/kesev). Note: this summary covers the opening portion of this section, which runs to p. 1550.
essay:
Now I'll expand the essay substantially while preserving all existing blockquotes, citations, and formatting. I'll add deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with source materials, and fuller treatment of theological and cosmological implications.

## Overview

This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 73 (pp. 1517–1550), a very long section (12,388 words). The text belongs to the *Secondo Cantico* (Tono Terzo) of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* and takes as its point of departure the Song of Songs verse in which the Bridegroom compares his Bride to a dove (*Amica mia, mia colomba, mia bella*). From this lyrical opening Zorzi launches a dense typological and allegorical exegesis of Levitical sacrifice, consistently harmonising Mosaic law, Christology, Neoplatonic faculty-psychology, and Renaissance Christian Kabbalah.

The section exemplifies Zorzi's fundamental hermeneutical principle: that Scripture operates on multiple simultaneous levels of meaning, each reflecting a higher plane of reality. The literal sense (the actual practices of Levitical priesthood) authenticates the moral sense (the psychology of human virtue and vice), which in turn yields to the allegorical (the cosmic work of Christ), and finally the anagogical (the mystical ascent of the soul toward divine union). This quadrifold exegetical method, inherited from Origen and the medieval Christian tradition, receives a distinctive Renaissance inflection when combined with Neoplatonic psychology and Kabbalistic cosmology.

---

## The Dove as Symbol of the Holy Spirit and of Christ the Purifier

The section opens with the pneumatological significance of the dove. Zorzi marshals the Gospel accounts of Christ's baptism (Matthew 3, John 1, Luke 3) to establish that the Holy Spirit chose no other animal for its epiphany. He refines the standard reading: the descent "like a dove" refers not necessarily to the Spirit taking a dove's bodily form, but to the manner of flight — swift, direct, sure — or to some intrinsic purity shared between the bird and the Third Person of the Trinity.

> Essa vola con una tale semplicità e rettitudine che il profeta desidera di essere trasportato a Dio sulle sue ali quando dice: Chi mi darà ali come di colomba, per volare e trovare riposo?

<cite>p. 1517</cite>

This distinction between the *modus* of descent and the *quidditas* of the Spirit's essence reflects Zorzi's careful negotiation between literal and mystical hermeneutics. The "like a dove" formula (*ὡς περιστερά* in Greek, *sicut columba* in Latin) permits multiple interpretative registers. At the literal level, it might suggest an actual avian shape; at the pneumatological level, it signifies the manner and character of the Spirit's movement — a movement marked by rectilinearity and simplicity, virtues opposed to the serpentine cunning (*prudentia serpentis*) that led to humanity's fall. The dove's flight, unlike that of the raven or the crow, does not scavenge or circle predatorily; it flies direct and swift to its destination, embodying the straightforward path of divine grace.

Zorzi here draws implicitly on the Neoplatonic hierarchy of beings. In the *Enneads*, Plotinus distinguishes between higher and lower manifestations of divine principle, each suited to its plane of reality. The dove, occupying a middle register between earthly baseness and purely spiritual form, becomes the perfect *symbolu* — the material vessel through which immaterial reality presses into manifestation. This is not mere poetic fancy but a considered metaphysical claim: the dove carries within its nature something of the divine simplicity it represents, and thus can serve as both sign and quasi-sacramental presence of the Holy Spirit's descent.

The dove's role in Levitical purification sacrifices (Lev. 12, the rite for a mother after childbirth) is then given a typological gloss: the pair of doves or turtledoves offered by the poor stands as a figure (*figura*) of the purification to be accomplished by Christ as the Supreme Pontiff in the Holy Spirit. Zorzi cites Paul (Gal. 4:6, predestination "according to the spirit of sanctification") and Isaiah 61:1 ("the Spirit of the Lord is upon me") as prophetic previews of this pneumatological purification. The turtledove (*turtur*) receives particular attention: it is not merely a bird, but a creature marked by constancy in love and mourning, its solitary song lamenting separation from its mate. This virtue of constancy becomes typologically significant — the turtledove represents the constancy of Israel's faith, and more fully, the faithful love of the Church as the Bride of Christ.

The significance of the purification after childbirth deserves fuller analysis. In Levitical logic, childbirth produces a state of ritual impurity not because the body is defiled, but because the natural processes of generation and bloodshed temporarily render the mother unable to approach the holy. The offering of doves restores her to communion with God. For Zorzi, this ritual conceals a profound theological truth: human nature, conceived in sin and born of fallen Eve, requires purification not through works or knowledge alone, but through the descending action of the Holy Spirit (*per lo Spirito Santo*), figured in the dove's descent at Christ's baptism. Every human birth, being entry into the fallen condition, must be spiritually reversed or redeemed by Christ's uncomplaining self-offering in the Spirit.

---

## Chapter 17: The Two Birds for the Leper's Cleansing (Leviticus 14)

Zorzi devotes sustained attention to the rite of the two birds for purifying leprosy. He begins with important Hebrew philology: the word *tzipporim* (צִפֳּרִים) is a generic term for birds, not specifically "sparrows" (*passeres*) as the Vulgate sometimes renders it; he adduces Origen's view that the birds in question may even have been hens. This philological point clears the way for typological interpretation.

> In effetti l'ebraico tzipporim significa genericamente uccelli, come possiamo desumere da molti passi della sacra scrittura e in particolare dal testo in questione, in cui è scritto: Ordina al sacerdote che prenda due tzipporim vivi, mondi.

<cite>p. 1351 (Italian text)</cite>

The attention to Hebrew terminology is not pedantic but methodologically crucial for Zorzi's entire hermeneutical project. The Kabbalists — and Zorzi stands in this tradition — hold that Hebrew, the language in which God spoke creation into being ("Let there be light"), contains within its very letters and phonemes the architectonic structure of reality. When the Vulgate, translating into Latin, renders *tzipporim* as "sparrows," it loses access to the generic quality crucial to the typology: the birds must be *any* birds, unpecified, precisely because they stand for human nature in its generality — neither specifically masculine nor feminine, neither proud nor lowly, simply "bird-nature" awaiting transformation.

Origen's commentary (cited here from Zorzi's recollection) proposes that the birds might be hens. This matters: a hen is domestic, productive, non-predatory — in short, a creature of useful service, not grandeur. Origen often emphasizes Christ's humility in becoming a victim rather than a mighty judge. The bird's ordinariness becomes part of its meaning. Humanity, too, is in itself ordinary, only elevated through participation in the Cosmic Christos.

The ritual itself (one bird killed over running water in an earthen vessel, the other dipped in its blood and released alive into the open field) is interpreted on two levels. At the literal/moral level, the two birds signify the two conditions of the leper's flesh — the partly living and the partly dead. At the allegorical level, the two birds are the two natures (*pignora*) offered by Christ for human expiation: the animal/corporeal man (the slaughtered bird, the body killed in the earthen vessel of flesh) and the spiritual man/soul (the bird released alive, the soul commended to the Father).

> Sembra, dunque, che quel paragone si riferisca piuttosto al volo o a una qualche proprietà che alla forma.

<cite>p. 1517</cite>

This bifold Christological reading reveals Zorzi's debt to Origen's doctrine of *theoria*, the spiritual vision that perceives divine truth clothed in material figures. Christ, the Word made flesh, paradoxically offers himself in both registers simultaneously: as the *Verbum carnis mortis* (the Word of death), the victim slain; and as the *Verbum vitae ascendens* (the Word of life ascending), the freed bird released toward heaven. The slaughter over running water is not accidental detail: the water signifies the perpetual flow of grace, the "living water" Christ offers to the Samaritan woman, and the blood of the slaughtered bird mingles with this flow, sanctifying all waters. The earthen vessel, too, speaks to Christ's incarnation — made of earth, of Adam's clay, destined to break, yet in its breaking releasing the redemptive power within.

The fact that one bird is released *alive* into the open field introduces a crucial note: resurrection and ascension are not merely concepts but living realities. The bird soars into the open (*in agrum apertum*), the unconstrained space where it may fly freely — figured as Christ's ascension into that realm beyond all creation where the risen body, perfected yet bearing its marks, dwells with the Father. Meanwhile, the corpse of the first bird remains over the earthen vessel, a sacrifice accepted, consumed, transformed into the very medium that purifies (the water receiving the blood). This is the paschal logic in miniature: death and life, sacrifice and resurrection, are not opposites but complementary movements within the single economy of salvation.

Zorzi reads the cedarmwood, scarlet, and hyssop (Lev. 14:4) with precision. The cedar stands for the Cross. The scarlet (*coccus*) is identified with the Hebrew *tola'at* (תּוֹלַעַת), the "worm" of Psalm 22 (*anoki tola'at*, "I am a worm and not a man"). To this is added *shani* (שָׁנִי), which Zorzi glosses not as "twice-dyed" but as "secondary" or "twin" (*geminus*) — perfectly apt for Christ's dual nature, divine and human. The hyssop (*libanotis*/rosemary) is praised as the noblest of plants and the most suited to aspersion, with its woody twigs and dense small leaves.

The color symbolism here participates in a long medieval Christian allegorical tradition, but Zorzi refines it through Kabbalistic numerology and Neoplatonic theory of colors. Scarlet, the color of life-blood, but also of shame and the scarlet letter of sin (Isaiah 1:18, "though your sins are like scarlet, they shall become white as snow"), embodies the paradox of redemptive suffering. The *tola'at* reading deepens this: in the creation of scarlet dye, a worm must be crushed, its body yielding the dye that marks the cloth. Christ is simultaneously the worm of Psalm 22 (the lowest, most despised creature) and the source of the scarlet dye that colors history. The *shani* or "twin" aspect signifies that this dual nature — worm and dyer, despised and powerful — is necessary to the work. The cedar, incorruptible wood, never decays; it signifies the eternal, unchanging divine nature against which the dual-natured redemptive act unfolds.

The hyssop deserves closer attention. In Jewish cultic practice, hyssop is the instrument of aspersion (sprinkling). When the high priest dips the hyssop in the mingled blood and water and strikes the leper seven times, he performs not a violent act but a consecrating one. The hyssop plant, with its many small leaves, multiplies the point of contact between the purifying fluid and the human flesh. This multiplication speaks to the universality of redemption: the one sacrifice, in its falling like dew through the many leaves of the hyssop-plant, reaches multitudes. Hyssop is also mentioned in Psalm 51:7 ("Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean") and in John 19:29, where the dying Christ is offered a sponge on a *hyssop branch*. For Zorzi, this is no coincidence: the same instrument that purifies the leper in the law purifies all Israel in the Psalms, and offers drink to the dying Savior in the Gospel. One thing, one plant, serves as the vehicle of purification across all times and modes.

The aspersion of the healed leper seven times is linked to the symbolism of the number seven as the number of remission and atonement. Seven is the number of the Sabbath, of rest, of fulfilled time. It is the number of the Jubilee (seven sevens of years), when all debts are forgiven and all slaves released. To sprinkle seven times is to enact the Jubilee over the leper's body: all his debts, all his separation from community, remitted in a single gesture. Zorzi closes the chapter with John the Apostle's vision: "Christ loved us and washed us from our sins in his blood," purifying the human race more effectively than the first creature (*protoplastes*, Adam) had infected it. Here the cosmic scope becomes explicit: the leprosy of sin, inherited from Adam, infected all humanity; the purification through Christ reverses that infection and restores the race to its pre-lapsarian condition — or rather, to a condition exceeding it, since the Resurrection dignifies the body in a way the original creation did not.

---

## Chapter 18: The Goat and the Bull Offered for Sin

Having treated purification rites, Zorzi turns to the sin-offerings. The goat (*hircus*, the father of kids) offered for sin represents the lascivious part of the human soul — the concupiscible faculty — likened to the goats placed on the Judge's left hand at the Last Judgment (Matt. 25). The vine-destroying foxes and kids of the Song of Songs are drawn in to reinforce the allegory of unruly appetite.

The goat's *beard* receives a long excursus. Drawing explicitly on Origen, Zorzi interprets the beard as symbolising "strength" or "virtue" of various kinds: the intellectual prowess of the Greeks, the astrological power of the Assyrians and Chaldaeans, the esoteric philosophy of the Persians and Egyptians (reaching as far as pacts with evil spirits), the military and financial power of temporal lords, and finally the generative power of carnal men. This ambivalent beard-symbolism is anchored in Isaiah 7 (the razor hired from beyond the river that will shave head, feet-hair, and beard — i.e., the king of Assyria) and in Deuteronomy (the captive foreign woman whose head is shaved and nails cut before she may be taken as wife, symbolising the stripping of gentile and carnal strength before election).

> La forza degli assiri e dei caldei nella conoscenza delle stelle e nell'influsso che deve esserne ricevuto. È, inoltre, la forza dei persiani e degli egiziani nella filosofia esoterica che si spinge fino a stringere patti con gli spiriti malvagi.

<cite>p. 1357 (Italian text)</cite>

This catalogue of beard-strengths reveals the deeply syncretic character of Zorzi's thought. He does not dismiss pagan knowledge or power but rather situates it on a graduated scale, from the legitimate (intellectual virtue of the Greeks) through the ambiguous (astrological influence) to the actively demonic (pacts with spirits). This is characteristic Renaissance Christian Kabbalah: the cosmos is unified, all knowledge and power flowing from divine emanation, but the same flows can be blocked, perverted, or inverted by human will. The Assyrian's wisdom in the stars is real power; the Persian's esoteric philosophy is real knowledge; but both, cut off from the living fountain in Christ, become corrupting influences.

The beard, as the sign of virility and mature strength, thus becomes the symbol of all such human prideful attainment. When the beard is shaved — when human presumption is stripped away — the creature stands naked before God. This stripping is not humiliation but preparation. The bride taken from among the vanquished nations, her head shaved and nails trimmed, becomes eligible for marriage into the covenant people because she has been divested of all false stronghold and made simple, like the dove.

The bull or ox (*vitulus/bos*) represents pride and vainglory (*fastus et superbia*) — the irascible faculty in its degenerate form. This faculty, in Platonic and Neoplatonic psychology, is the spirited (*thumoeidès*) part of the soul, the seat of courage, anger, and the will-to-power. In itself, thymos is necessary: without it, the soul would lack the fortitude to resist bodily appetites and march toward virtue. But in the fallen state, thymos becomes inflated, turns inward upon itself, seeks dominion and honor for its own sake. The bull, strongest of sacrificial beasts, horned and bellowing, represents this misdirected spiritual force. To offer the bull is to acknowledge that the very source of one's strength — the capacity for assertion, for mastery, for self-overcoming — must be laid down before God, else it becomes demonic.

Together, lust and pride correspond to the two main "fountains" of the inferior man: the concupiscible and the irascible. Origen's commentary on Numbers is quoted at length: Moses (the knowledge of the law) and Aaron (the skill of sacrifice) must both act together — neither alone suffices — to lead the elect out of Egypt and through the desert. Moses without Aaron is mere law, cold and condemning; Aaron without Moses is ritual without wisdom, empty form. Christ fulfils both roles, offering himself as the living sacrifice for bull, goat, and every holocaust, "because he is the whole and contains all things." This phrase — *because he is the whole and contains all things* — echoes Neoplatonic language for the One, the indivisible source from which all differentiation flows. Christ is the One in whom all contrary forces (law and grace, justice and mercy, power and humility) are reconciled.

The theological implication extends further. In Kabbalistic cosmology, the Godhead emanates downward through the *Sephiroth* (the ten spheres or emanations), passing through increasingly dense and differentiated levels. At each level, a particular divine attribute or name becomes manifest. The Fall is understood as a breaking of the vessels (*shevirah hakelim*): the lower worlds cannot contain the infinite light, and so it breaks and scatters. The work of restoration (*tikkun*) requires that each broken attribute — represented here by the goat and bull — be redeemed and elevated back toward its source. Christ's sacrifice accomplishes this restoration universally and definitively: every demonic distortion of power and desire is drawn back into alignment with their proper divine source.

The chapter ends with the grain-of-wheat saying (John 12:24), applied to Christ mortifying "the beard" — the strength of the inferior man — in himself as Head, so that it might be mortified in all members. The image of the grain falling into the ground, dying, and bearing much fruit speaks to the necessary death of the ego-will for the flowering of true spiritual abundance. Christ, the Head of the cosmic body, first accomplishes this mortification in himself — he empties himself (*kenosis*), becoming obedient unto death. And in doing so, he opens a way for all members of his body to follow: "If you wish to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross."

---

## Chapter 19: The Sheep and the Lamb

The final chapter in the sampled portion examines why sheep and lambs are prescribed specifically for purification after childbirth and for peace-offerings (*pro pace*). Zorzi opens with philology: the Hebrew *tzo'n* (צֹאן) covers the entire ovine family, while *keves* (כֶּבֶשׂ) or *kesev* (כֶּשֶׂב) designates a young lamb of either sex — two terms used, always with mystery, interchangeably in Scripture.

The key theological point is that sheep and lambs originate from a place higher (*ab altiori loco*) than the right hand of the Judge where they are finally placed — an allusion to Zorzi's Kabbalistic cosmology of divine emanation. In the Last Judgment scene (Matt. 25), the sheep are placed on the right hand of the throne, the place of favor. But these sheep, though they end exalted, begin from a lofty origin: their innocence and gentleness are not earned virtues but participatory reflections of the divine simplicity. They are not ambitious, not proud, not lusting — they embody the *natura innocentium*, innocent nature untouched by the corruption that paradise-knowledge brings.

Christ himself is described "now as lamb, now as sheep" (John the Baptist's *Ecce Agnus Dei*; Isaiah 53:7). The term *agnus* (lamb) emphasizes innocence and youth, vulnerability and sacrifice. The term *ovis* (sheep) emphasizes membership in a flock, participation in community, willingness to follow the shepherd. In the Psalms, the faithful are frequently imaged as sheep following their shepherd; in the Gospels, Christ is the Shepherd and also the Lamb. This apparent paradox dissolves when understood Kabbalististically: Christ contains all roles, all emanations. As the Shepherd (*roeh*), he represents the active, protective, governing principle; as the Lamb (*taleh*, *carmel*), he represents the passive, yielding, sacrificial principle. Both are necessary to the work of redemption.

The section celebrates the great biblical shepherds — Abel, Abraham, Jacob, Moses, David — as figures anticipating the Good Shepherd who lays down his soul for his sheep. Each of these worthies exemplifies a particular mode of shepherding. Abel, the first, offers his flock with a pure heart and is exalted; Abraham shelters and feeds his flocks and is called the friend of God; Jacob serves years for his flocks and receives divine blessings; Moses guides Israel as a shepherd guides sheep; David, risen from shepherd-boy to king, composes the Psalms in which the God-shepherd relationship is most tenderly expressed. Each is a *praefiguratio* (prefiguring) of Christ, the Chief Shepherd, in whose person all their virtues are unified and infinitized.

> Si addice dunque alla pecora, vero tipo di Colui che rappacificò tutte le cose con il sangue della sua croce, venire offerta per la pacificazione dell'uomo con Dio. Nessun altro, infatti, poteva rappacificarci altrettanto bene con il sommo Padre come Colui che è nel seno del Padre.

<cite>pp. 1363–1364 (Italian text)</cite>

The logic here is cosmologically profound. Reconciliation (*pacificatio*) is not merely the forgetting of offenses; it is the restoration of right relationship between levels of reality. Humanity, made in God's image yet fallen into pride and lust, has broken covenant. The gap is not merely moral but metaphysical: the human will, bent away from its proper good, cannot by its own power turn back toward the divine source. Only one who stands in both realms — fully human yet fully divine, fully innocent yet fully sacrificial — can bridge that chasm. The sheep, innocent and gentle, is the only creature fit to stand as *figura* for such a mediator.

The peace-offering (*shelem*, *pro pace*) deserves deeper consideration. Unlike the sin-offering (which addresses transgression) or the burnt-offering (which addresses the demand for total consecration), the peace-offering celebrates communion, restored relationship, shared meal. When the peace-offering is completed, portions are returned to the offerer to be eaten in a sacred meal with family and guests. This is the only sacrifice in which the offerer and the community participate by consuming the flesh. The lamb, then, when offered for peace, becomes the substance of the reunion feast. Christ's body, broken and shared in the Eucharist, is the supreme peace-offering: his flesh and blood, once consumed, establish the community of the Church, a communion (*communio*) sealed in his flesh.

---

## Sources and Method

Zorzi's principal explicit sources in this passage are: Origen (commentary on Numbers, and on Leviticus); Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (on the angels' question "Who is this coming from Edom?" — Isaiah 63); Isaiah (passim, especially chapters 7, 53, 61, 63); the Psalms (Ps. 22, the *tola'at* passage); Paul (Galatians 4:6, Romans 12:1); and the Four Gospels. The Hebrew philological notes (tzipporim, tola'at, shani, tzo'n, keves) are characteristic of Zorzi's Christian Kabbalist methodology — demonstrating that the inner logic of the Mosaic rites is only fully legible through a combination of literal Hebrew lexicography and Christological allegory. The Platonic/Origenist concupiscible-irascible faculty schema provides the psychological architecture into which the sacrificial animals are mapped.

Zorzi's synthesis achieves something historically remarkable: he takes the entire Levitical sacrificial system, which by his time was superseded and replaced in Christian theology by Christ's once-for-all sacrifice, and reveals it as a hidden transcript in which that very supersession is prophetically encoded. The goat and bull are not arbitrary choices for different sins; they correspond to the two faculties of the inferior soul in the Neoplatonic scheme. The cedarmwood, scarlet, hyssop, and water of the purification rite are not ceremonial decorations but a kind of material theology, expressing through the visible world the invisible work of redemption. The sheep and lamb, ascending from a high source yet placed finally on the right hand of the Judge, embody the paradox of the Incarnation itself — the descent of the divine into flesh, and the exaltation of flesh into glory.

This hermeneutical vision is not arbitrary allegory (where any animal could signify any vice, depending on whim) but rather what Aquinas would call *analogia entis* — analogy of being — where the creature, precisely in its nature and habits, participates in and reflects divine truth. The dove truly is simple and direct in its flight; the goat truly is lustful and bearded in its strength; the sheep truly is innocent and communal. These natures are not imposed from outside but read from within, and in being read, reveal the cosmic drama in which all natures play their appointed part. The Mosaic law, when so read, ceases to be a dead letter or a type awaiting the antitype; it becomes living wisdom, speaking in every detail of the one Word by whom and through whom all things were made.
